{{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Thunder gods]] [[Category:War gods]] [[Category:Earthquake myths]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Takemikazuchi]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Takemikazuchi]] [[en:Takemikazuchi]] {{DEFAULTSORT:takemikazuchi}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Japanese thunder god}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | image = Namazu4.jpg | caption = Earthquake-warding song (a ''[[namazu-e]]'' woodblock pamphlet, October 1855). The figure holding down the [[Namazu]] (mythical [[catfish]]) is believed to be Takemikazuchi.<ref>{{Harvnb|小向|1992}}, p.77 carries a comparable namazu-e ([http://shinku.nichibun.ac.jp/namazu/sakuhin.php?id=17 鹿島要石真図], which identifies the god as Takemikazuchi</ref> | father = [[Kagu-tsuchi]] / [[Ame-no-ohabari]] }}
{{Nihongo|'''Takemikazuchi'''|建御雷/武甕槌}} is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]], considered a [[list of thunder gods|god of thunder]]<ref name=ashkenazi>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|publisher=ABC-CLIO|year=2003|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration|page=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/266 266]|isbn=9781576074671}}</ref> and a sword god.<ref name=heibonsha-takem>{{citation|last=三品|first=彰英 (Shōei Mishina)|volume=14|title=たけみかづち|page=367|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref> He also competed in what is considered the first [[sumo]] wrestling match recorded in history.
He is otherwise known as "The ''kami'' of Kashima" ('''Kashima-no-kami'''), the chief deity revered in the [[Kashima Shrine]] at [[Kashima, Ibaraki]] (and all other subsidiary Kashima shrines).<ref name=ashkenazi/><ref name=Ouwehand/> In the ''[[namazu-e]]'' or catfish prints of the [[Edo period]], Takemikazuchi/Kashima is depicted attempting to subdue the [[Namazu]], a giant catfish supposedly dwelling at the {{Nihongo|''[[kaname-ishi]]''|{{wikt-lang|ja|要石}}||'pinning rock'}} of the Japanese landmass and causing its [[earthquake]]s.<ref name=ashkenazi/><ref name=Ouwehand/>
==Forms of the name== In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', the god is known as Takemikazuchi-no-o no kami (建御雷之男神 – "Brave Mighty Thunderbolt Man").<ref name="Heldt">Heldt, Gustav. ''The Kojiki: An Account of Ancient Matters''. Columbia University Press, 2014.</ref> He also bears the alternate names {{Nihongo|'''Takefutsu-no-kami'''|建布都神}} and {{Nihongo|'''Toyofutsu-no-kami'''|豊布都神}}.<ref name=chamberlin-fire-deity>{{Harvnb|Chamberlain|1919|loc=§VIII, The slaying of the fire-deity, pp.35–}}</ref><ref name=kojiki-kamiumi>{{Harvnb|武田|1996}}『古事記』text p. 27/ mod. Ja. tr. p.213</ref>
===Birth of the gods=== In the [[Kamiumi]] ("birth of the gods") episodes of the ''Kojiki'', the god of creation [[Izanagi]] severs the head of the fire deity [[Kagu-tsuchi]], whereupon the blood from the sword ([[Totsuka-no-Tsurugi]]) splattered the rocks and gave birth to several deities. The blood from the sword-tip engendered one triad of deities, and the blood from near the base of the blade produced another triad that included Takemikazuchi (here given as "Brave-Awful-Possessing-Male-Deity" by Chamberlain).<ref name=chamberlin-fire-deity/><ref name=kojiki-kamiumi/>
The name of the ten-fist sword wielded by Izanagi is given postscripturally as [[Ame-no-ohabari]], otherwise known as Itsu-no-ohabari.<ref name=Ouwehand>{{cite book|last1=Ouwehand |first1=Cornelis |last2=Logunova |first2=Vera Vasil'evna|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion |publisher=Brill Archive|year=1964|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA57|pages=57–}}, gives sword as "Itsu-no-o habari"</ref> (Accordingly, Takemikazuchi is referred in some passages as the child of Itsu-no-o habari. See [[#Quelling of the Middle Country|next section]]).
The ''Nihon Shoki'' gives the same episode in the same general gist, albeit more vaguely regarding this deity.<ref name=aston-birth>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, pp.28–29;</ref><ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.26</ref>
===Quelling of the Middle Country=== [[File:Takemikazuchi-pins-Namazu-with-Kaname-ishi-spirit-stone-1855.png|thumb|Takemikazuchi pins down a catfish (''namazu'') with a rock (''kaname-ishi'') to prevent earthquakes, 1855]] [[File:Kashima-kanameishi-shinzu-namazu-e.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Another ''[[Namazu-e]]'' depicting Takemikazuchi riding the catfish. Above is a depiction of the ''kaname-ishi'', the rock used to subdue the fish]]
In the episodes where the gods of the heavenly plains ([[Takama-ga-hara]]) contemplate and execute the conquest of the terrestrial world known as Middle Country ([[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]]), Takemikazuchi is one of the chief delegates sent down to subjugate the terrestrial deities (''kuni-tsu-kami'').
In the ''Kojiki'' (Conquest of [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] chapter), the heavenly deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi|Takamusubi]] decreed that either Takemikazuchi or his father [[Ame-no-ohabari|Itsu-no-ohabari]] ("Heaven-Point-Blade-Extended") must be sent down for the conquest. Itsu-no-ohabari (who appeared previously as a ten-fist sword) here has the mind and speech of a sentient god, and he volunteered his son Takemikazuchi for the subjugation campaign. Takemikazuchi was accompanied by {{Interlanguage link multi|Ame-no-torifune|ja|3=鳥之石楠船神|vertical-align=sup}} "Deity Heavenly-Bird-Boat" (which may be a boat as well as being a god)<ref name=chamberlin-fire-deity2>{{Harvnb|Chamberlain|1919|loc=§XXXII, Abdication of the deity Master-of-the-Great-Land, pp.121-}}</ref><ref name=kojiki-kuniyuzuri>{{Harvnb|武田|1996}}『古事記』text p. 60/ mod. Ja. tr. p.244</ref>
The two deities reached the land of [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] at a place called {{Nihongo|"the little shore of Izasa/Inasa"|伊耶佐小浜}}, and stuck a "ten-fist sword" ([[Totsuka-no-Tsurugi]]) upside-down on the crest of the wave, and sat atop it, while demanding the local god [[Ōkuninushi]] to relinquish the Izumo province over to them. Ōkuninushi replied he would defer the decision to his child deities, and would follow suit in their counsel. One of them, [[Kotoshironushi]] or Yae-Kotoshironushi ("Eight-Fold-Thing-Sign-Master") who had been out fishing, was easily persuaded to forfeit his authority and retire into seclusion.
The other, [[Takeminakata]] would not concede without testing his feats of strength against Takemikazuchi. When the challenger grabbed Takemikazuchi's hand it turned as if into an icicle and then a sword, making him cringe. Takemikazuchi then grabbed Takeminakata's hand, crushing it like a young reed.<ref name=ashkenazi/> The challenger, chased to the sea near {{Nihongo|Suwa of Shinano|科野国之州羽海|extra=in ''Kojiki''}}, asked for clemency on his life, promising to hold himself in exile in that region (in this way, the defeated Takeminakata became chief deity of the [[Suwa taisha|Suwa Grand Shrine]] in [[Nagano Prefecture]]).<ref name=ashkenazi/><ref name=kojiki-kuniyuzuri/>
The hand-to-hand bout between the two deities is considered the mythical origin of [[sumo]] wrestling.<ref>{{citation|last=彦山|first=光三 (Mitsuzō Hikoyama)|volume=12|title=すもう|page=597|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki'' names a different partner for Takemikazuchi in the task of conquering lands of the Middle Country. That partner is '''[[Futsunushi]]''' (a god who goes unmentioned in the ''Kojiki'' in the gods' birth episode<ref name=Ouwehand/> as well as this episode).<ref name=aston-izumo1>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, Chapter:Age of the Gods II, pp.67-70.</ref><ref name=nihonshoki-izumo1>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.56-8</ref>
Just as Takemikazuchi was chief deity of [[Kashima Shrine]], this Futsunushi was the chief of the [[Katori Shrine]].<ref name=Ouwehand/><ref name=heibonsha-kashima>{{citation|last=武田|first=政一 (Masaichi Takeda)|volume=4|title=かしまじんじゃ|page=404|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref> In the early centuries, when the Yamato rulers campaigned in the [[Kantō]] and [[Tōhoku]] regions, they would pray to these two war gods for military success, so that subsidiary shrines of the two gods are scattered all over these regions.<ref name=heibonsha-kashima/> The enshrinement of the deities at Kashima and Katori is mentioned briefly in the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' (807).<ref name=Ouwehand/>
The ''Nihon Shoki'' account has other discrepancies. The beach where the gods stuck the "ten-fist sword" is here called "Itasa". The chief god of Izumo (Ōkuninushi) is called by the name of Ōanamuchi.<ref group=notes>Oho-na-mochi in {{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}</ref> The wrestling match with Takeminakata is missing. In the end, Ōanamuchi/Ōkuninushi gave sign of his obedience by presenting the broad spear he used to pacify the land with.<ref name=aston-izumo1/><ref name=nihonshoki-izumo1/> Jumping to a later passage (after digressing on other matters), the ''Nihon Shoki'' retells Takemikazuchi and Futsunushi's landing on the beach, this time stating that Ōanamuchi verbally expressed resistance to relinquish his rule, until the heavenly gods promised him palatial residence to recompense his abdication.<ref name=aston-izumo2>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, Chapter:Age of the Gods II, pp.79-80.</ref><ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64-6</ref>
Appended to the two passages is the mention of a star deity named [[Amatsu-Mikaboshi]] who resisted till the end, and whom Takemikazuchi and Futsunushi were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be Futsunushi.<ref>{{cite book|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|title=和漢三才図会|year=1985 |volume=10|publisher=平凡社|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|page=71|isbn=9784582804478 }} subscribes to this view</ref> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|12=draft}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref>
===Emperor Jimmu's conquest of the East=== {{Main|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}} Takemikazuchi's sword aided [[Emperor Jimmu]] in his subjugation of the east. At [[Kumano Region|Kumano]], the Emperor and his troops were either struck unconscious by the appearance of a bear (''Kojiki'')<ref name=kojiki-takakuraji>{{Harvnb|武田|1996}}『古事記』text p. 77-8/ mod. Ja. tr. p.260-1</ref><ref name=chamberlin-cross-sword>{{Harvnb|Chamberlain|1919|loc=§XLV.—Emperor Jim-mu (Part II.—The Cross-Sword Sent Down From Heaven)., pp.164-}}</ref> or severely debilitated by the poison fumes spewed out by local gods (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name=aston-birth2>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896|pp=114-115}}</ref><ref name=nihongi-takakuraji>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}}, p.94-5</ref> But a man named Takakuraji presented a gift of a sword, the emperor awoke, and without him hardly brandishing this weapon, the evil deities of Kumano were spontaneously cut down. When Jimmu inquired, Takkuraji explained that he had a vision in a dream where the supreme deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi|Takamusubi]] were about to command Takemikazuchi to descend to earth once again to pacify the lands, this time to assist the emperor. However, Takemizuchi replied that it would be sufficient to send down the sword he used during his campaigns, and, boring a hole through Takakuraji's storehouse, deposited the sword, bidding the man to present it to Emperor Jimmu. That sword bore the names of {{Nihongo|{{ill|Futsu-no-mitama|ja|布都御魂|12=draft}}|[[:ja:布都御魂|布都御魂]]}}, {{Nihongo|Saji-futsu-no-kami|佐士布都神}}, and {{Nihongo|Mika-futsu no kami|甕布都神}}.<ref name=chamberlin-cross-sword/> This sword is the main dedication ([[goshintai]]) kept at [[Isonokami Shrine]].<ref name=kojiki-takakuraji/>
==Commentary== <!-- (This family tree image in Japanese)[[Image:Creation myths of Japan 3.svg|thumb|right|250px|Kamiumi or gods' birth mythology. [[media:Creation myths of Japan 3.svg|SVG view for compatible browsers]]]]--> <!--etymology 名前の「ミカヅチ」はイカヅチ 雷 に接頭語「ミ」をつけた「ミ・イカヅチ」の縮まったものであり、雷神は剣の神でもある。また、別名のフツ神は本来は別の神で、『日本書紀』では葦原中国平定でタケミカヅチとともに降ったのは 経津主神 であると記されている。経津主神は 香取神宮 で祀られている神である。-->
According to {{Interlanguage link multi|Iwao Ōwa|ja|3=大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup}} in his ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989), Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''[[kunitsukami]]'') revered by the {{Nihongo|[[Ō clan]]|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏}}}},<ref name=gakken/> and was a god of maritime travel.<ref name=gakken/> However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}).<ref name=gakken>{{cite book|last=小向|first=正司|publisher=学研|year=1992|title=神道の本|series=Books Esoterica|volume=2|pages=76–7}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000)</ref>
The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name=gakken/> (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.)
When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty.
==In popular culture== * Takemikazuchi is a supporting character in the popular manga and anime series ''[[Noragami]]''. * Takemikazuchi is the leader of the Takemikazuchi Familia in the manga and anime series ''[[Is It Wrong to Try to Pick Up Girls in a Dungeon?|DanMachi]]''. * In the video game ''[[Persona 4]]'', Take-Mikazuchi is [[Kanji Tatsumi]]'s initial Persona. * Takemikazuchi is the name of one of the Supreme Beings in the ''[[Overlord (novel series)|Overlord]]'' series. * There is a ''[[Digimon]]'' named Kazuchimon. Along with another ''[[Digimon]]'' named Fenriloogamon, both can DNA digivolve into its combined form, known as Fenriloogamon: Takemikazuchi. * In the videogame ''[[Naruto Shippuden: Ultimate Ninja Storm 4]]'', [[Sasuke Uchiha]] can use an Ultimate Move called "Takemikazuchi". * In the [[visual novel]] [[Muv-Luv Alternative]] Type-00 Takemikazuchi is the name of the best Tactical Surface Fighter mech of the Imperial Japanese Mainland Defence Force, only being accessible to the Imperial Royal Guard pilots.
==See also== *[[Kamiumi]] *[[List of Japanese deities]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese thunder god}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | image = Namazu4.jpg | caption = Earthquake-warding song (a ''[[namazu-e]]'' woodblock pamphlet, October 1855). The figure holding down the [[Namazu]] (mythical [[catfish]]) is believed to be Takemikazuchi.<ref>{{Harvnb|小向|1992}}, p.77 carries a comparable namazu-e ([http://shinku.nichibun.ac.jp/namazu/sakuhin.php?id=17 鹿島要石真図], which identifies the god as Takemikazuchi</ref> | father = [[Kagu-tsuchi]] / [[Ame-no-ohabari]] }}
{{Nihongo|'''Takemikazuchi'''|建御雷/武甕槌}} is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]], considered a [[list of thunder gods|god of thunder]]<ref name=ashkenazi>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|publisher=ABC-CLIO|year=2003|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration|page=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/266 266]|isbn=9781576074671}}</ref> and a sword god.<ref name=heibonsha-takem>{{citation|last=三品|first=彰英 (Shōei Mishina)|volume=14|title=たけみかづち|page=367|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref> He also competed in what is considered the first [[sumo]] wrestling match recorded in history.
He is otherwise known as "The ''kami'' of Kashima" ('''Kashima-no-kami'''), the chief deity revered in the [[Kashima Shrine]] at [[Kashima, Ibaraki]] (and all other subsidiary Kashima shrines).<ref name=ashkenazi/><ref name=Ouwehand/> In the ''[[namazu-e]]'' or catfish prints of the [[Edo period]], Takemikazuchi/Kashima is depicted attempting to subdue the [[Namazu]], a giant catfish supposedly dwelling at the {{Nihongo|''[[kaname-ishi]]''|{{wikt-lang|ja|要石}}||'pinning rock'}} of the Japanese landmass and causing its [[earthquake]]s.<ref name=ashkenazi/><ref name=Ouwehand/>
==Forms of the name== In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', the god is known as Takemikazuchi-no-o no kami (建御雷之男神 – "Brave Mighty Thunderbolt Man").<ref name="Heldt">Heldt, Gustav. ''The Kojiki: An Account of Ancient Matters''. Columbia University Press, 2014.</ref> He also bears the alternate names {{Nihongo|'''Takefutsu-no-kami'''|建布都神}} and {{Nihongo|'''Toyofutsu-no-kami'''|豊布都神}}.<ref name=chamberlin-fire-deity>{{Harvnb|Chamberlain|1919|loc=§VIII, The slaying of the fire-deity, pp.35–}}</ref><ref name=kojiki-kamiumi>{{Harvnb|武田|1996}}『古事記』text p. 27/ mod. Ja. tr. p.213</ref>
===Birth of the gods=== In the [[Kamiumi]] ("birth of the gods") episodes of the ''Kojiki'', the god of creation [[Izanagi]] severs the head of the fire deity [[Kagu-tsuchi]], whereupon the blood from the sword ([[Totsuka-no-Tsurugi]]) splattered the rocks and gave birth to several deities. The blood from the sword-tip engendered one triad of deities, and the blood from near the base of the blade produced another triad that included Takemikazuchi (here given as "Brave-Awful-Possessing-Male-Deity" by Chamberlain).<ref name=chamberlin-fire-deity/><ref name=kojiki-kamiumi/>
The name of the ten-fist sword wielded by Izanagi is given postscripturally as [[Ame-no-ohabari]], otherwise known as Itsu-no-ohabari.<ref name=Ouwehand>{{cite book|last1=Ouwehand |first1=Cornelis |last2=Logunova |first2=Vera Vasil'evna|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion |publisher=Brill Archive|year=1964|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA57|pages=57–}}, gives sword as "Itsu-no-o habari"</ref> (Accordingly, Takemikazuchi is referred in some passages as the child of Itsu-no-o habari. See [[#Quelling of the Middle Country|next section]]).
The ''Nihon Shoki'' gives the same episode in the same general gist, albeit more vaguely regarding this deity.<ref name=aston-birth>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, pp.28–29;</ref><ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.26</ref>
===Quelling of the Middle Country=== [[File:Takemikazuchi-pins-Namazu-with-Kaname-ishi-spirit-stone-1855.png|thumb|Takemikazuchi pins down a catfish (''namazu'') with a rock (''kaname-ishi'') to prevent earthquakes, 1855]] [[File:Kashima-kanameishi-shinzu-namazu-e.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Another ''[[Namazu-e]]'' depicting Takemikazuchi riding the catfish. Above is a depiction of the ''kaname-ishi'', the rock used to subdue the fish]]
In the episodes where the gods of the heavenly plains ([[Takama-ga-hara]]) contemplate and execute the conquest of the terrestrial world known as Middle Country ([[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]]), Takemikazuchi is one of the chief delegates sent down to subjugate the terrestrial deities (''kuni-tsu-kami'').
In the ''Kojiki'' (Conquest of [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] chapter), the heavenly deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi|Takamusubi]] decreed that either Takemikazuchi or his father [[Ame-no-ohabari|Itsu-no-ohabari]] ("Heaven-Point-Blade-Extended") must be sent down for the conquest. Itsu-no-ohabari (who appeared previously as a ten-fist sword) here has the mind and speech of a sentient god, and he volunteered his son Takemikazuchi for the subjugation campaign. Takemikazuchi was accompanied by {{Interlanguage link multi|Ame-no-torifune|ja|3=鳥之石楠船神|vertical-align=sup}} "Deity Heavenly-Bird-Boat" (which may be a boat as well as being a god)<ref name=chamberlin-fire-deity2>{{Harvnb|Chamberlain|1919|loc=§XXXII, Abdication of the deity Master-of-the-Great-Land, pp.121-}}</ref><ref name=kojiki-kuniyuzuri>{{Harvnb|武田|1996}}『古事記』text p. 60/ mod. Ja. tr. p.244</ref>
The two deities reached the land of [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] at a place called {{Nihongo|"the little shore of Izasa/Inasa"|伊耶佐小浜}}, and stuck a "ten-fist sword" ([[Totsuka-no-Tsurugi]]) upside-down on the crest of the wave, and sat atop it, while demanding the local god [[Ōkuninushi]] to relinquish the Izumo province over to them. Ōkuninushi replied he would defer the decision to his child deities, and would follow suit in their counsel. One of them, [[Kotoshironushi]] or Yae-Kotoshironushi ("Eight-Fold-Thing-Sign-Master") who had been out fishing, was easily persuaded to forfeit his authority and retire into seclusion.
The other, [[Takeminakata]] would not concede without testing his feats of strength against Takemikazuchi. When the challenger grabbed Takemikazuchi's hand it turned as if into an icicle and then a sword, making him cringe. Takemikazuchi then grabbed Takeminakata's hand, crushing it like a young reed.<ref name=ashkenazi/> The challenger, chased to the sea near {{Nihongo|Suwa of Shinano|科野国之州羽海|extra=in ''Kojiki''}}, asked for clemency on his life, promising to hold himself in exile in that region (in this way, the defeated Takeminakata became chief deity of the [[Suwa taisha|Suwa Grand Shrine]] in [[Nagano Prefecture]]).<ref name=ashkenazi/><ref name=kojiki-kuniyuzuri/>
The hand-to-hand bout between the two deities is considered the mythical origin of [[sumo]] wrestling.<ref>{{citation|last=彦山|first=光三 (Mitsuzō Hikoyama)|volume=12|title=すもう|page=597|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki'' names a different partner for Takemikazuchi in the task of conquering lands of the Middle Country. That partner is '''[[Futsunushi]]''' (a god who goes unmentioned in the ''Kojiki'' in the gods' birth episode<ref name=Ouwehand/> as well as this episode).<ref name=aston-izumo1>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, Chapter:Age of the Gods II, pp.67-70.</ref><ref name=nihonshoki-izumo1>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.56-8</ref>
Just as Takemikazuchi was chief deity of [[Kashima Shrine]], this Futsunushi was the chief of the [[Katori Shrine]].<ref name=Ouwehand/><ref name=heibonsha-kashima>{{citation|last=武田|first=政一 (Masaichi Takeda)|volume=4|title=かしまじんじゃ|page=404|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref> In the early centuries, when the Yamato rulers campaigned in the [[Kantō]] and [[Tōhoku]] regions, they would pray to these two war gods for military success, so that subsidiary shrines of the two gods are scattered all over these regions.<ref name=heibonsha-kashima/> The enshrinement of the deities at Kashima and Katori is mentioned briefly in the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' (807).<ref name=Ouwehand/>
The ''Nihon Shoki'' account has other discrepancies. The beach where the gods stuck the "ten-fist sword" is here called "Itasa". The chief god of Izumo (Ōkuninushi) is called by the name of Ōanamuchi.<ref group=notes>Oho-na-mochi in {{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}</ref> The wrestling match with Takeminakata is missing. In the end, Ōanamuchi/Ōkuninushi gave sign of his obedience by presenting the broad spear he used to pacify the land with.<ref name=aston-izumo1/><ref name=nihonshoki-izumo1/> Jumping to a later passage (after digressing on other matters), the ''Nihon Shoki'' retells Takemikazuchi and Futsunushi's landing on the beach, this time stating that Ōanamuchi verbally expressed resistance to relinquish his rule, until the heavenly gods promised him palatial residence to recompense his abdication.<ref name=aston-izumo2>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, Chapter:Age of the Gods II, pp.79-80.</ref><ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64-6</ref>
Appended to the two passages is the mention of a star deity named [[Amatsu-Mikaboshi]] who resisted till the end, and whom Takemikazuchi and Futsunushi were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be Futsunushi.<ref>{{cite book|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|title=和漢三才図会|year=1985 |volume=10|publisher=平凡社|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|page=71|isbn=9784582804478 }} subscribes to this view</ref> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|12=draft|12=draft}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref>
===Emperor Jimmu's conquest of the East=== {{Main|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}} Takemikazuchi's sword aided [[Emperor Jimmu]] in his subjugation of the east. At [[Kumano Region|Kumano]], the Emperor and his troops were either struck unconscious by the appearance of a bear (''Kojiki'')<ref name=kojiki-takakuraji>{{Harvnb|武田|1996}}『古事記』text p. 77-8/ mod. Ja. tr. p.260-1</ref><ref name=chamberlin-cross-sword>{{Harvnb|Chamberlain|1919|loc=§XLV.—Emperor Jim-mu (Part II.—The Cross-Sword Sent Down From Heaven)., pp.164-}}</ref> or severely debilitated by the poison fumes spewed out by local gods (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name=aston-birth2>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896|pp=114-115}}</ref><ref name=nihongi-takakuraji>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}}, p.94-5</ref> But a man named Takakuraji presented a gift of a sword, the emperor awoke, and without him hardly brandishing this weapon, the evil deities of Kumano were spontaneously cut down. When Jimmu inquired, Takkuraji explained that he had a vision in a dream where the supreme deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi|Takamusubi]] were about to command Takemikazuchi to descend to earth once again to pacify the lands, this time to assist the emperor. However, Takemizuchi replied that it would be sufficient to send down the sword he used during his campaigns, and, boring a hole through Takakuraji's storehouse, deposited the sword, bidding the man to present it to Emperor Jimmu. That sword bore the names of {{Nihongo|{{ill|Futsu-no-mitama|ja|布都御魂|12=draft|12=draft}}|[[:ja:布都御魂|布都御魂]]}}, {{Nihongo|Saji-futsu-no-kami|佐士布都神}}, and {{Nihongo|Mika-futsu no kami|甕布都神}}.<ref name=chamberlin-cross-sword/> This sword is the main dedication ([[goshintai]]) kept at [[Isonokami Shrine]].<ref name=kojiki-takakuraji/>
==Commentary==
According to {{Interlanguage link multi|Iwao Ōwa|ja|3=大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup}} in his ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989), Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''[[kunitsukami]]'') revered by the {{Nihongo|[[Ō clan]]|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏}}}},<ref name=gakken/> and was a god of maritime travel.<ref name=gakken/> However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}).<ref name=gakken>{{cite book|last=小向|first=正司|publisher=学研|year=1992|title=神道の本|series=Books Esoterica|volume=2|pages=76–7}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000)</ref>
The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name=gakken/> (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.)
When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty.
==In popular culture== * Takemikazuchi is a supporting character in the popular manga and anime series ''[[Noragami]]''. * Takemikazuchi is the leader of the Takemikazuchi Familia in the manga and anime series ''[[Is It Wrong to Try to Pick Up Girls in a Dungeon?|DanMachi]]''. * In the video game ''[[Persona 4]]'', Take-Mikazuchi is [[Kanji Tatsumi]]'s initial Persona. * Takemikazuchi is the name of one of the Supreme Beings in the ''[[Overlord (novel series)|Overlord]]'' series. * There is a ''[[Digimon]]'' named Kazuchimon. Along with another ''[[Digimon]]'' named Fenriloogamon, both can DNA digivolve into its combined form, known as Fenriloogamon: Takemikazuchi. * In the videogame ''[[Naruto Shippuden: Ultimate Ninja Storm 4]]'', [[Sasuke Uchiha]] can use an Ultimate Move called "Takemikazuchi". * In the [[visual novel]] [[Muv-Luv Alternative]] Type-00 Takemikazuchi is the name of the best Tactical Surface Fighter mech of the Imperial Japanese Mainland Defence Force, only being accessible to the Imperial Royal Guard pilots.

The figure holding down the [[Namazu]] (mythical [[catfish]]) is believed to be Takemikazuchi.<ref>{{Harvnb|小向|1992}}, p.77 carries a comparable namazu-e ([http://shinku.nichibun.ac.jp/namazu/sakuhin.php?id=17 鹿島要石真図], which identifies the god as Takemikazuchi</ref> | father = [[Kagu-tsuchi]] / [[Ame-no-ohabari]] }}
{{Nihongo|'''Takemikazuchi'''|建御雷/武甕槌}} is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]], considered a [[list of thunder gods|god of thunder]]<ref name=ashkenazi>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|publisher=ABC-CLIO|year=2003|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration|page=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/266 266]|isbn=9781576074671}}</ref> and a sword god.<ref name=heibonsha-takem>{{citation|last=三品|first=彰英 (Shōei Mishina)|volume=14|title=たけみかづち|page=367|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref> He also competed in what is considered the first [[sumo]] wrestling match recorded in history.
He is otherwise known as "The ''kami'' of Kashima" ('''Kashima-no-kami'''), the chief deity revered in the [[Kashima Shrine]] at [[Kashima, Ibaraki]] (and all other subsidiary Kashima shrines).<ref name=ashkenazi/><ref name=Ouwehand/> In the ''[[namazu-e]]'' or catfish prints of the [[Edo period]], Takemikazuchi/Kashima is depicted attempting to subdue the [[Namazu]], a giant catfish supposedly dwelling at the {{Nihongo|''[[kaname-ishi]]''|{{wikt-lang|ja|要石}}||'pinning rock'}} of the Japanese landmass and causing its [[earthquake]]s.<ref name=ashkenazi/><ref name=Ouwehand/>
==Forms of the name== In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', the god is known as Takemikazuchi-no-o no kami (建御雷之男神 – "Brave Mighty Thunderbolt Man").<ref name="Heldt">Heldt, Gustav. ''The Kojiki: An Account of Ancient Matters''. Columbia University Press, 2014.</ref> He also bears the alternate names {{Nihongo|'''Takefutsu-no-kami'''|建布都神}} and {{Nihongo|'''Toyofutsu-no-kami'''|豊布都神}}.<ref name=chamberlin-fire-deity>{{Harvnb|Chamberlain|1919|loc=§VIII, The slaying of the fire-deity, pp.35–}}</ref><ref name=kojiki-kamiumi>{{Harvnb|武田|1996}}『古事記』text p. 27/ mod. Ja. tr. p.213</ref>
===Birth of the gods=== In the [[Kamiumi]] ("birth of the gods") episodes of the ''Kojiki'', the god of creation [[Izanagi]] severs the head of the fire deity [[Kagu-tsuchi]], whereupon the blood from the sword ([[Totsuka-no-Tsurugi]]) splattered the rocks and gave birth to several deities. The blood from the sword-tip engendered one triad of deities, and the blood from near the base of the blade produced another triad that included Takemikazuchi (here given as "Brave-Awful-Possessing-Male-Deity" by Chamberlain).<ref name=chamberlin-fire-deity/><ref name=kojiki-kamiumi/>
The name of the ten-fist sword wielded by Izanagi is given postscripturally as [[Ame-no-ohabari]], otherwise known as Itsu-no-ohabari.<ref name=Ouwehand>{{cite book|last1=Ouwehand |first1=Cornelis |last2=Logunova |first2=Vera Vasil'evna|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion |publisher=Brill Archive|year=1964|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA57|pages=57–}}, gives sword as "Itsu-no-o habari"</ref> (Accordingly, Takemikazuchi is referred in some passages as the child of Itsu-no-o habari. See [[#Quelling of the Middle Country|next section]]).
The ''Nihon Shoki'' gives the same episode in the same general gist, albeit more vaguely regarding this deity.<ref name=aston-birth>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, pp.28–29;</ref><ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.26</ref>
===Quelling of the Middle Country=== [[File:Takemikazuchi-pins-Namazu-with-Kaname-ishi-spirit-stone-1855.png|thumb|Takemikazuchi pins down a catfish (''namazu'') with a rock (''kaname-ishi'') to prevent earthquakes, 1855]] [[File:Kashima-kanameishi-shinzu-namazu-e.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Another ''[[Namazu-e]]'' depicting Takemikazuchi riding the catfish. Above is a depiction of the ''kaname-ishi'', the rock used to subdue the fish]]
In the episodes where the gods of the heavenly plains ([[Takama-ga-hara]]) contemplate and execute the conquest of the terrestrial world known as Middle Country ([[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]]), Takemikazuchi is one of the chief delegates sent down to subjugate the terrestrial deities (''kuni-tsu-kami'').
In the ''Kojiki'' (Conquest of [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] chapter), the heavenly deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi|Takamusubi]] decreed that either Takemikazuchi or his father [[Ame-no-ohabari|Itsu-no-ohabari]] ("Heaven-Point-Blade-Extended") must be sent down for the conquest. Itsu-no-ohabari (who appeared previously as a ten-fist sword) here has the mind and speech of a sentient god, and he volunteered his son Takemikazuchi for the subjugation campaign. Takemikazuchi was accompanied by {{Interlanguage link multi|Ame-no-torifune|ja|3=鳥之石楠船神|vertical-align=sup}} "Deity Heavenly-Bird-Boat" (which may be a boat as well as being a god)<ref name=chamberlin-fire-deity2>{{Harvnb|Chamberlain|1919|loc=§XXXII, Abdication of the deity Master-of-the-Great-Land, pp.121-}}</ref><ref name=kojiki-kuniyuzuri>{{Harvnb|武田|1996}}『古事記』text p. 60/ mod. Ja. tr. p.244</ref>
The two deities reached the land of [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] at a place called {{Nihongo|"the little shore of Izasa/Inasa"|伊耶佐小浜}}, and stuck a "ten-fist sword" ([[Totsuka-no-Tsurugi]]) upside-down on the crest of the wave, and sat atop it, while demanding the local god [[Ōkuninushi]] to relinquish the Izumo province over to them. Ōkuninushi replied he would defer the decision to his child deities, and would follow suit in their counsel. One of them, [[Kotoshironushi]] or Yae-Kotoshironushi ("Eight-Fold-Thing-Sign-Master") who had been out fishing, was easily persuaded to forfeit his authority and retire into seclusion.
The other, [[Takeminakata]] would not concede without testing his feats of strength against Takemikazuchi. When the challenger grabbed Takemikazuchi's hand it turned as if into an icicle and then a sword, making him cringe. Takemikazuchi then grabbed Takeminakata's hand, crushing it like a young reed.<ref name=ashkenazi/> The challenger, chased to the sea near {{Nihongo|Suwa of Shinano|科野国之州羽海|extra=in ''Kojiki''}}, asked for clemency on his life, promising to hold himself in exile in that region (in this way, the defeated Takeminakata became chief deity of the [[Suwa taisha|Suwa Grand Shrine]] in [[Nagano Prefecture]]).<ref name=ashkenazi/><ref name=kojiki-kuniyuzuri/>
The hand-to-hand bout between the two deities is considered the mythical origin of [[sumo]] wrestling.<ref>{{citation|last=彦山|first=光三 (Mitsuzō Hikoyama)|volume=12|title=すもう|page=597|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki'' names a different partner for Takemikazuchi in the task of conquering lands of the Middle Country. That partner is '''[[Futsunushi]]''' (a god who goes unmentioned in the ''Kojiki'' in the gods' birth episode<ref name=Ouwehand/> as well as this episode).<ref name=aston-izumo1>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, Chapter:Age of the Gods II, pp.67-70.</ref><ref name=nihonshoki-izumo1>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.56-8</ref>
Just as Takemikazuchi was chief deity of [[Kashima Shrine]], this Futsunushi was the chief of the [[Katori Shrine]].<ref name=Ouwehand/><ref name=heibonsha-kashima>{{citation|last=武田|first=政一 (Masaichi Takeda)|volume=4|title=かしまじんじゃ|page=404|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref> In the early centuries, when the Yamato rulers campaigned in the [[Kantō]] and [[Tōhoku]] regions, they would pray to these two war gods for military success, so that subsidiary shrines of the two gods are scattered all over these regions.<ref name=heibonsha-kashima/> The enshrinement of the deities at Kashima and Katori is mentioned briefly in the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' (807).<ref name=Ouwehand/>
The ''Nihon Shoki'' account has other discrepancies. The beach where the gods stuck the "ten-fist sword" is here called "Itasa". The chief god of Izumo (Ōkuninushi) is called by the name of Ōanamuchi.<ref group=notes>Oho-na-mochi in {{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}</ref> The wrestling match with Takeminakata is missing. In the end, Ōanamuchi/Ōkuninushi gave sign of his obedience by presenting the broad spear he used to pacify the land with.<ref name=aston-izumo1/><ref name=nihonshoki-izumo1/> Jumping to a later passage (after digressing on other matters), the ''Nihon Shoki'' retells Takemikazuchi and Futsunushi's landing on the beach, this time stating that Ōanamuchi verbally expressed resistance to relinquish his rule, until the heavenly gods promised him palatial residence to recompense his abdication.<ref name=aston-izumo2>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, Chapter:Age of the Gods II, pp.79-80.</ref><ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64-6</ref>
Appended to the two passages is the mention of a star deity named [[Amatsu-Mikaboshi]] who resisted till the end, and whom Takemikazuchi and Futsunushi were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be Futsunushi.<ref>{{cite book|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|title=和漢三才図会|year=1985 |volume=10|publisher=平凡社|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|page=71|isbn=9784582804478 }} subscribes to this view</ref> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref>
===Emperor Jimmu's conquest of the East=== {{Main|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}} Takemikazuchi's sword aided [[Emperor Jimmu]] in his subjugation of the east. At [[Kumano Region|Kumano]], the Emperor and his troops were either struck unconscious by the appearance of a bear (''Kojiki'')<ref name=kojiki-takakuraji>{{Harvnb|武田|1996}}『古事記』text p. 77-8/ mod. Ja. tr. p.260-1</ref><ref name=chamberlin-cross-sword>{{Harvnb|Chamberlain|1919|loc=§XLV.—Emperor Jim-mu (Part II.—The Cross-Sword Sent Down From Heaven)., pp.164-}}</ref> or severely debilitated by the poison fumes spewed out by local gods (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name=aston-birth2>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896|pp=114-115}}</ref><ref name=nihongi-takakuraji>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}}, p.94-5</ref> But a man named Takakuraji presented a gift of a sword, the emperor awoke, and without him hardly brandishing this weapon, the evil deities of Kumano were spontaneously cut down. When Jimmu inquired, Takkuraji explained that he had a vision in a dream where the supreme deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi|Takamusubi]] were about to command Takemikazuchi to descend to earth once again to pacify the lands, this time to assist the emperor. However, Takemizuchi replied that it would be sufficient to send down the sword he used during his campaigns, and, boring a hole through Takakuraji's storehouse, deposited the sword, bidding the man to present it to Emperor Jimmu. That sword bore the names of {{Nihongo|{{ill|Futsu-no-mitama|ja|布都御魂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|[[:ja:布都御魂|布都御魂]]}}, {{Nihongo|Saji-futsu-no-kami|佐士布都神}}, and {{Nihongo|Mika-futsu no kami|甕布都神}}.<ref name=chamberlin-cross-sword/> This sword is the main dedication ([[goshintai]]) kept at [[Isonokami Shrine]].<ref name=kojiki-takakuraji/>
==Commentary==
According to {{Interlanguage link multi|Iwao Ōwa|ja|3=大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup}} in his ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989), Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''[[kunitsukami]]'') revered by the {{Nihongo|[[Ō clan]]|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏}}}},<ref name=gakken/> and was a god of maritime travel.<ref name=gakken/> However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}).<ref name=gakken>{{cite book|last=小向|first=正司|publisher=学研|year=1992|title=神道の本|series=Books Esoterica|volume=2|pages=76–7}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000)</ref>
The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name=gakken/> (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.)
When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty.
==In popular culture== * Takemikazuchi is a supporting character in the popular manga and anime series ''[[Noragami]]''. * Takemikazuchi is the leader of the Takemikazuchi Familia in the manga and anime series ''[[Is It Wrong to Try to Pick Up Girls in a Dungeon?|DanMachi]]''. * In the video game ''[[Persona 4]]'', Take-Mikazuchi is [[Kanji Tatsumi]]'s initial Persona. * Takemikazuchi is the name of one of the Supreme Beings in the ''[[Overlord (novel series)|Overlord]]'' series. * There is a ''[[Digimon]]'' named Kazuchimon. Along with another ''[[Digimon]]'' named Fenriloogamon, both can DNA digivolve into its combined form, known as Fenriloogamon: Takemikazuchi. * In the videogame ''[[Naruto Shippuden: Ultimate Ninja Storm 4]]'', [[Sasuke Uchiha]] can use an Ultimate Move called "Takemikazuchi". * In the [[visual novel]] [[Muv-Luv Alternative]] Type-00 Takemikazuchi is the name of the best Tactical Surface Fighter mech of the Imperial Japanese Mainland Defence Force, only being accessible to the Imperial Royal Guard pilots.

{{Short description|Japanese thunder god}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | image = Namazu4.jpg | caption = Earthquake-warding song (a ''[[namazu-e]]'' woodblock pamphlet, October 1855). The figure holding down the [[Namazu]] (mythical [[catfish]]) is believed to be Takemikazuchi.<ref>{{Harvnb|小向|1992}}, p.77 carries a comparable namazu-e ([http://shinku.nichibun.ac.jp/namazu/sakuhin.php?id=17 鹿島要石真図], which identifies the god as Takemikazuchi</ref> | father = [[Kagu-tsuchi]] / [[Ame-no-ohabari]] }}

Above is a depiction of the ''kaname-ishi'', the rock used to subdue the fish]]

The hand-to-hand bout between the two deities is considered the mythical origin of [[sumo]] wrestling.<ref>{{citation|last=彦山|first=光三 (Mitsuzō Hikoyama)|volume=12|title=すもう|page=597|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki'' names a different partner for Takemikazuchi in the task of conquering lands of the Middle Country. That partner is '''[[Futsunushi]]''' (a god who goes unmentioned in the ''Kojiki'' in the gods' birth episode<ref name=Ouwehand/> as well as this episode).<ref name=aston-izumo1>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, Chapter:Age of the Gods II, pp.67-70.</ref><ref name=nihonshoki-izumo1>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.56-8</ref>
Just as Takemikazuchi was chief deity of [[Kashima Shrine]], this Futsunushi was the chief of the [[Katori Shrine]].<ref name=Ouwehand/><ref name=heibonsha-kashima>{{citation|last=武田|first=政一 (Masaichi Takeda)|volume=4|title=かしまじんじゃ|page=404|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref> In the early centuries, when the Yamato rulers campaigned in the [[Kantō]] and [[Tōhoku]] regions, they would pray to these two war gods for military success, so that subsidiary shrines of the two gods are scattered all over these regions.<ref name=heibonsha-kashima/> The enshrinement of the deities at Kashima and Katori is mentioned briefly in the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' (807).<ref name=Ouwehand/>
The ''Nihon Shoki'' account has other discrepancies. The beach where the gods stuck the "ten-fist sword" is here called "Itasa". The chief god of Izumo (Ōkuninushi) is called by the name of Ōanamuchi.<ref group=notes>Oho-na-mochi in {{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}</ref> The wrestling match with Takeminakata is missing. In the end, Ōanamuchi/Ōkuninushi gave sign of his obedience by presenting the broad spear he used to pacify the land with.<ref name=aston-izumo1/><ref name=nihonshoki-izumo1/> Jumping to a later passage (after digressing on other matters), the ''Nihon Shoki'' retells Takemikazuchi and Futsunushi's landing on the beach, this time stating that Ōanamuchi verbally expressed resistance to relinquish his rule, until the heavenly gods promised him palatial residence to recompense his abdication.<ref name=aston-izumo2>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, Chapter:Age of the Gods II, pp.79-80.</ref><ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64-6</ref>
Appended to the two passages is the mention of a star deity named [[Amatsu-Mikaboshi]] who resisted till the end, and whom Takemikazuchi and Futsunushi were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be Futsunushi.<ref>{{cite book|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|title=和漢三才図会|year=1985 |volume=10|publisher=平凡社|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|page=71|isbn=9784582804478 }} subscribes to this view</ref> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref>
===Emperor Jimmu's conquest of the East=== {{Main|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}} Takemikazuchi's sword aided [[Emperor Jimmu]] in his subjugation of the east. At [[Kumano Region|Kumano]], the Emperor and his troops were either struck unconscious by the appearance of a bear (''Kojiki'')<ref name=kojiki-takakuraji>{{Harvnb|武田|1996}}『古事記』text p. 77-8/ mod. Ja. tr. p.260-1</ref><ref name=chamberlin-cross-sword>{{Harvnb|Chamberlain|1919|loc=§XLV.—Emperor Jim-mu (Part II.—The Cross-Sword Sent Down From Heaven)., pp.164-}}</ref> or severely debilitated by the poison fumes spewed out by local gods (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name=aston-birth2>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896|pp=114-115}}</ref><ref name=nihongi-takakuraji>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}}, p.94-5</ref> But a man named Takakuraji presented a gift of a sword, the emperor awoke, and without him hardly brandishing this weapon, the evil deities of Kumano were spontaneously cut down. When Jimmu inquired, Takkuraji explained that he had a vision in a dream where the supreme deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi|Takamusubi]] were about to command Takemikazuchi to descend to earth once again to pacify the lands, this time to assist the emperor. However, Takemizuchi replied that it would be sufficient to send down the sword he used during his campaigns, and, boring a hole through Takakuraji's storehouse, deposited the sword, bidding the man to present it to Emperor Jimmu. That sword bore the names of {{Nihongo|{{ill|Futsu-no-mitama|ja|布都御魂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|[[:ja:布都御魂|布都御魂]]}}, {{Nihongo|Saji-futsu-no-kami|佐士布都神}}, and {{Nihongo|Mika-futsu no kami|甕布都神}}.<ref name=chamberlin-cross-sword/> This sword is the main dedication ([[goshintai]]) kept at [[Isonokami Shrine]].<ref name=kojiki-takakuraji/>
==Commentary==
According to {{Interlanguage link multi|Iwao Ōwa|ja|3=大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup}} in his ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989), Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''[[kunitsukami]]'') revered by the {{Nihongo|[[Ō clan]]|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏}}}},<ref name=gakken/> and was a god of maritime travel.<ref name=gakken/> However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}).<ref name=gakken>{{cite book|last=小向|first=正司|publisher=学研|year=1992|title=神道の本|series=Books Esoterica|volume=2|pages=76–7}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000)</ref>
The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name=gakken/> (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.)
When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty.
==In popular culture== * Takemikazuchi is a supporting character in the popular manga and anime series ''[[Noragami]]''. * Takemikazuchi is the leader of the Takemikazuchi Familia in the manga and anime series ''[[Is It Wrong to Try to Pick Up Girls in a Dungeon?|DanMachi]]''. * In the video game ''[[Persona 4]]'', Take-Mikazuchi is [[Kanji Tatsumi]]'s initial Persona. * Takemikazuchi is the name of one of the Supreme Beings in the ''[[Overlord (novel series)|Overlord]]'' series. * There is a ''[[Digimon]]'' named Kazuchimon. Along with another ''[[Digimon]]'' named Fenriloogamon, both can DNA digivolve into its combined form, known as Fenriloogamon: Takemikazuchi. * In the videogame ''[[Naruto Shippuden: Ultimate Ninja Storm 4]]'', [[Sasuke Uchiha]] can use an Ultimate Move called "Takemikazuchi". * In the [[visual novel]] [[Muv-Luv Alternative]] Type-00 Takemikazuchi is the name of the best Tactical Surface Fighter mech of the Imperial Japanese Mainland Defence Force, only being accessible to the Imperial Royal Guard pilots.

<!-- (This family tree image in Japanese)[[Image:Creation myths of Japan 3.svg|thumb|right|250px|Kamiumi or gods' birth mythology. [[media:Creation myths of Japan 3.svg|SVG view for compatible browsers]]]]--> <!--etymology 名前の「ミカヅチ」はイカヅチ 雷 に接頭語「ミ」をつけた「ミ・イカヅチ」の縮まったものであり、雷神は剣の神でもある。また、別名のフツ神は本来は別の神で、『日本書紀』では葦原中国平定でタケミカヅチとともに降ったのは 経津主神 であると記されている。経津主神は 香取神宮 で祀られている神である。-->

{{Short description|Japanese thunder god}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | image = Namazu4.jpg | caption = Earthquake-warding song (a ''[[namazu-e]]'' woodblock pamphlet, October 1855). The figure holding down the [[Namazu]] (mythical [[catfish]]) is believed to be Takemikazuchi.<ref>{{Harvnb|小向|1992}}, p.77 carries a comparable namazu-e ([http://shinku.nichibun.ac.jp/namazu/sakuhin.php?id=17 鹿島要石真図], which identifies the god as Takemikazuchi</ref> | father = [[Kagu-tsuchi]] / [[Ame-no-ohabari]] }}
{{Nihongo|'''Takemikazuchi'''|建御雷/武甕槌}} is a deity in [[Japanese mythology]], considered a [[list of thunder gods|god of thunder]]<ref name=ashkenazi>{{cite book|last=Ashkenazi|first=Michael|title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology|publisher=ABC-CLIO|year=2003|url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk|url-access=registration|page=[https://archive.org/details/handbookofjapane0000ashk/page/266 266]|isbn=9781576074671}}</ref> and a sword god.<ref name=heibonsha-takem>{{citation|last=三品|first=彰英 (Shōei Mishina)|volume=14|title=たけみかづち|page=367|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref> He also competed in what is considered the first [[sumo]] wrestling match recorded in history.
He is otherwise known as "The ''kami'' of Kashima" ('''Kashima-no-kami'''), the chief deity revered in the [[Kashima Shrine]] at [[Kashima, Ibaraki]] (and all other subsidiary Kashima shrines).<ref name=ashkenazi/><ref name=Ouwehand/> In the ''[[namazu-e]]'' or catfish prints of the [[Edo period]], Takemikazuchi/Kashima is depicted attempting to subdue the [[Namazu]], a giant catfish supposedly dwelling at the {{Nihongo|''[[kaname-ishi]]''|{{wikt-lang|ja|要石}}||'pinning rock'}} of the Japanese landmass and causing its [[earthquake]]s.<ref name=ashkenazi/><ref name=Ouwehand/>
==Forms of the name== In the ''[[Kojiki]]'', the god is known as Takemikazuchi-no-o no kami (建御雷之男神 – "Brave Mighty Thunderbolt Man").<ref name="Heldt">Heldt, Gustav. ''The Kojiki: An Account of Ancient Matters''. Columbia University Press, 2014.</ref> He also bears the alternate names {{Nihongo|'''Takefutsu-no-kami'''|建布都神}} and {{Nihongo|'''Toyofutsu-no-kami'''|豊布都神}}.<ref name=chamberlin-fire-deity>{{Harvnb|Chamberlain|1919|loc=§VIII, The slaying of the fire-deity, pp.35–}}</ref><ref name=kojiki-kamiumi>{{Harvnb|武田|1996}}『古事記』text p. 27/ mod. Ja. tr. p.213</ref>
===Birth of the gods=== In the [[Kamiumi]] ("birth of the gods") episodes of the ''Kojiki'', the god of creation [[Izanagi]] severs the head of the fire deity [[Kagu-tsuchi]], whereupon the blood from the sword ([[Totsuka-no-Tsurugi]]) splattered the rocks and gave birth to several deities. The blood from the sword-tip engendered one triad of deities, and the blood from near the base of the blade produced another triad that included Takemikazuchi (here given as "Brave-Awful-Possessing-Male-Deity" by Chamberlain).<ref name=chamberlin-fire-deity/><ref name=kojiki-kamiumi/>
The name of the ten-fist sword wielded by Izanagi is given postscripturally as [[Ame-no-ohabari]], otherwise known as Itsu-no-ohabari.<ref name=Ouwehand>{{cite book|last1=Ouwehand |first1=Cornelis |last2=Logunova |first2=Vera Vasil'evna|title=Namazu-e and Their Themes: An Interpretative Approach to Some Aspects of Japanese Folk Religion |publisher=Brill Archive|year=1964|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1P4UAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA57|pages=57–}}, gives sword as "Itsu-no-o habari"</ref> (Accordingly, Takemikazuchi is referred in some passages as the child of Itsu-no-o habari. See [[#Quelling of the Middle Country|next section]]).
The ''Nihon Shoki'' gives the same episode in the same general gist, albeit more vaguely regarding this deity.<ref name=aston-birth>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, pp.28–29;</ref><ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.26</ref>
===Quelling of the Middle Country=== [[File:Takemikazuchi-pins-Namazu-with-Kaname-ishi-spirit-stone-1855.png|thumb|Takemikazuchi pins down a catfish (''namazu'') with a rock (''kaname-ishi'') to prevent earthquakes, 1855]] [[File:Kashima-kanameishi-shinzu-namazu-e.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Another ''[[Namazu-e]]'' depicting Takemikazuchi riding the catfish. Above is a depiction of the ''kaname-ishi'', the rock used to subdue the fish]]
In the episodes where the gods of the heavenly plains ([[Takama-ga-hara]]) contemplate and execute the conquest of the terrestrial world known as Middle Country ([[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni]]), Takemikazuchi is one of the chief delegates sent down to subjugate the terrestrial deities (''kuni-tsu-kami'').
In the ''Kojiki'' (Conquest of [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] chapter), the heavenly deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi|Takamusubi]] decreed that either Takemikazuchi or his father [[Ame-no-ohabari|Itsu-no-ohabari]] ("Heaven-Point-Blade-Extended") must be sent down for the conquest. Itsu-no-ohabari (who appeared previously as a ten-fist sword) here has the mind and speech of a sentient god, and he volunteered his son Takemikazuchi for the subjugation campaign. Takemikazuchi was accompanied by {{Interlanguage link multi|Ame-no-torifune|ja|3=鳥之石楠船神|vertical-align=sup}} "Deity Heavenly-Bird-Boat" (which may be a boat as well as being a god)<ref name=chamberlin-fire-deity2>{{Harvnb|Chamberlain|1919|loc=§XXXII, Abdication of the deity Master-of-the-Great-Land, pp.121-}}</ref><ref name=kojiki-kuniyuzuri>{{Harvnb|武田|1996}}『古事記』text p. 60/ mod. Ja. tr. p.244</ref>
The two deities reached the land of [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] at a place called {{Nihongo|"the little shore of Izasa/Inasa"|伊耶佐小浜}}, and stuck a "ten-fist sword" ([[Totsuka-no-Tsurugi]]) upside-down on the crest of the wave, and sat atop it, while demanding the local god [[Ōkuninushi]] to relinquish the Izumo province over to them. Ōkuninushi replied he would defer the decision to his child deities, and would follow suit in their counsel. One of them, [[Kotoshironushi]] or Yae-Kotoshironushi ("Eight-Fold-Thing-Sign-Master") who had been out fishing, was easily persuaded to forfeit his authority and retire into seclusion.
The other, [[Takeminakata]] would not concede without testing his feats of strength against Takemikazuchi. When the challenger grabbed Takemikazuchi's hand it turned as if into an icicle and then a sword, making him cringe. Takemikazuchi then grabbed Takeminakata's hand, crushing it like a young reed.<ref name=ashkenazi/> The challenger, chased to the sea near {{Nihongo|Suwa of Shinano|科野国之州羽海|extra=in ''Kojiki''}}, asked for clemency on his life, promising to hold himself in exile in that region (in this way, the defeated Takeminakata became chief deity of the [[Suwa taisha|Suwa Grand Shrine]] in [[Nagano Prefecture]]).<ref name=ashkenazi/><ref name=kojiki-kuniyuzuri/>
The hand-to-hand bout between the two deities is considered the mythical origin of [[sumo]] wrestling.<ref>{{citation|last=彦山|first=光三 (Mitsuzō Hikoyama)|volume=12|title=すもう|page=597|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki'' names a different partner for Takemikazuchi in the task of conquering lands of the Middle Country. That partner is '''[[Futsunushi]]''' (a god who goes unmentioned in the ''Kojiki'' in the gods' birth episode<ref name=Ouwehand/> as well as this episode).<ref name=aston-izumo1>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, Chapter:Age of the Gods II, pp.67-70.</ref><ref name=nihonshoki-izumo1>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.56-8</ref>
Just as Takemikazuchi was chief deity of [[Kashima Shrine]], this Futsunushi was the chief of the [[Katori Shrine]].<ref name=Ouwehand/><ref name=heibonsha-kashima>{{citation|last=武田|first=政一 (Masaichi Takeda)|volume=4|title=かしまじんじゃ|page=404|work=世界百科事典 (Sekai hyakka jiten)|publisher=Heibonsha|orig-year=1968|year=1969}}</ref> In the early centuries, when the Yamato rulers campaigned in the [[Kantō]] and [[Tōhoku]] regions, they would pray to these two war gods for military success, so that subsidiary shrines of the two gods are scattered all over these regions.<ref name=heibonsha-kashima/> The enshrinement of the deities at Kashima and Katori is mentioned briefly in the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'' (807).<ref name=Ouwehand/>
The ''Nihon Shoki'' account has other discrepancies. The beach where the gods stuck the "ten-fist sword" is here called "Itasa". The chief god of Izumo (Ōkuninushi) is called by the name of Ōanamuchi.<ref group=notes>Oho-na-mochi in {{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}</ref> The wrestling match with Takeminakata is missing. In the end, Ōanamuchi/Ōkuninushi gave sign of his obedience by presenting the broad spear he used to pacify the land with.<ref name=aston-izumo1/><ref name=nihonshoki-izumo1/> Jumping to a later passage (after digressing on other matters), the ''Nihon Shoki'' retells Takemikazuchi and Futsunushi's landing on the beach, this time stating that Ōanamuchi verbally expressed resistance to relinquish his rule, until the heavenly gods promised him palatial residence to recompense his abdication.<ref name=aston-izumo2>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896}}, Chapter:Age of the Gods II, pp.79-80.</ref><ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64-6</ref>
Appended to the two passages is the mention of a star deity named [[Amatsu-Mikaboshi]] who resisted till the end, and whom Takemikazuchi and Futsunushi were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be Futsunushi.<ref>{{cite book|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|title=和漢三才図会|year=1985 |volume=10|publisher=平凡社|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|page=71|isbn=9784582804478 }} subscribes to this view</ref> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|1=Takehazuchi|2=ja|3=天羽槌雄神|4=zh|5=天羽槌雄神|6=fr|7=Takehazuchi|lt=Takehazuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref>
===Emperor Jimmu's conquest of the East=== {{Main|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}} Takemikazuchi's sword aided [[Emperor Jimmu]] in his subjugation of the east. At [[Kumano Region|Kumano]], the Emperor and his troops were either struck unconscious by the appearance of a bear (''Kojiki'')<ref name=kojiki-takakuraji>{{Harvnb|武田|1996}}『古事記』text p. 77-8/ mod. Ja. tr. p.260-1</ref><ref name=chamberlin-cross-sword>{{Harvnb|Chamberlain|1919|loc=§XLV.—Emperor Jim-mu (Part II.—The Cross-Sword Sent Down From Heaven)., pp.164-}}</ref> or severely debilitated by the poison fumes spewed out by local gods (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name=aston-birth2>{{Harvnb|Aston|1896|pp=114-115}}</ref><ref name=nihongi-takakuraji>{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}}, p.94-5</ref> But a man named Takakuraji presented a gift of a sword, the emperor awoke, and without him hardly brandishing this weapon, the evil deities of Kumano were spontaneously cut down. When Jimmu inquired, Takkuraji explained that he had a vision in a dream where the supreme deities [[Amaterasu]] and [[Takamimusubi|Takamusubi]] were about to command Takemikazuchi to descend to earth once again to pacify the lands, this time to assist the emperor. However, Takemizuchi replied that it would be sufficient to send down the sword he used during his campaigns, and, boring a hole through Takakuraji's storehouse, deposited the sword, bidding the man to present it to Emperor Jimmu. That sword bore the names of {{Nihongo|{{ill|Futsu-no-mitama|ja|布都御魂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|[[:ja:布都御魂|布都御魂]]}}, {{Nihongo|Saji-futsu-no-kami|佐士布都神}}, and {{Nihongo|Mika-futsu no kami|甕布都神}}.<ref name=chamberlin-cross-sword/> This sword is the main dedication ([[goshintai]]) kept at [[Isonokami Shrine]].<ref name=kojiki-takakuraji/>
==Commentary==
According to {{Interlanguage link multi|Iwao Ōwa|ja|3=大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup}} in his ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989), Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''[[kunitsukami]]'') revered by the {{Nihongo|[[Ō clan]]|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏}}}},<ref name=gakken/> and was a god of maritime travel.<ref name=gakken/> However, the [[Nakatomi clan]] also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they claimed Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Ōwa goes on to theorize that the Ō clan was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"}}, but was usurped by the Nakatomi who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]}}).<ref name=gakken>{{cite book|last=小向|first=正司|publisher=学研|year=1992|title=神道の本|series=Books Esoterica|volume=2|pages=76–7}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000)</ref>
The Nakatomi clan, essentially the priestly branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]], also placed the veneration of Takemikazuchi in the [[Kasuga-taisha]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]].<ref name=gakken/> (The thunder god is one of several gods enshrined.)
When the [[Yamato kingship]] expanded control into the easterly dominions, Kashima ([[Kashima, Ibaraki]]) became a crucial base. Yamato armies and generals often prayed to the Kashima and Katori deities for military success against the intransigents in the east. In these ways, Takemikazuchi became an important deity for the imperial dynasty.
==In popular culture== * Takemikazuchi is a supporting character in the popular manga and anime series ''[[Noragami]]''. * Takemikazuchi is the leader of the Takemikazuchi Familia in the manga and anime series ''[[Is It Wrong to Try to Pick Up Girls in a Dungeon?|DanMachi]]''. * In the video game ''[[Persona 4]]'', Take-Mikazuchi is [[Kanji Tatsumi]]'s initial Persona. * Takemikazuchi is the name of one of the Supreme Beings in the ''[[Overlord (novel series)|Overlord]]'' series. * There is a ''[[Digimon]]'' named Kazuchimon. Along with another ''[[Digimon]]'' named Fenriloogamon, both can DNA digivolve into its combined form, known as Fenriloogamon: Takemikazuchi. * In the videogame ''[[Naruto Shippuden: Ultimate Ninja Storm 4]]'', [[Sasuke Uchiha]] can use an Ultimate Move called "Takemikazuchi". * In the [[visual novel]] [[Muv-Luv Alternative]] Type-00 Takemikazuchi is the name of the best Tactical Surface Fighter mech of the Imperial Japanese Mainland Defence Force, only being accessible to the Imperial Royal Guard pilots.
